Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd study when I felt like it, which wasn't often. Maybe I'd put in a few hours here and there throughout the week, mostly late at night when I couldn't sleep. I know nine hours a week isn't much, but I've always been able to absorb information pretty quickly when I actually sit down to do it. The lack of structure didn't bother me as much as it probably should have.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go days without looking at anything, then maybe cram a bit when something was due or when I remembered I was enrolled in these courses. Deadlines would sometimes get me moving, but even then, I wasn't consistent. Life just felt heavy, you know? Some weeks I had more energy than others, and that's what determined when I studied more than any schedule did.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I got an email notification about something or when I vaguely remembered I should probably check what was happening. Sometimes it was just boredom, scrolling through my phone and thinking I might as well see what's on the LMS. It wasn't driven by any real enthusiasm or plan. I'd log in, look around for a bit, maybe watch part of a video, then drift away again.
Q4: Not really, no. It never became a habit for me. There were brief periods where I'd log in more regularly, maybe when a gamified task caught my attention for a moment, but it never stuck. I just couldn't make myself care enough to build it into my daily routine. The platform was there, I used it when I had to, but it never felt like something I wanted to return to.
Q5: The lecture videos were the most useful for me because I learn best by listening. I could put them on and absorb the information without having to read through walls of text. Sometimes I'd have them playing while doing other things, and the concepts would still sink in. The discussion boards were okay too, gave me a sense of what other people were thinking, but I rarely contributed much myself.
Q6: I ignored most of the reading materials and a lot of the assignments, to be honest. My assignment completion was terrible, I know that. It's not that I didn't understand the importance, I just couldn't bring myself to do them. The motivation wasn't there. I'd open a PDF, stare at it, feel this weight in my chest, and close it. Some resources just felt like too much effort for where my head was at.
Q7: Something felt worth my time if it was quick and gave me what I needed without too much effort. If I could listen to something and understand the core concept in twenty minutes, that was perfect. The gamified tasks were interesting at first because they broke up the monotony, but even those lost their appeal after a while. Basically, low effort with high payoff was what I was looking for.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt completely skippable to me. If something wasn't required, I almost never did it. Required activities at least had some external pressure attached, even if I often didn't complete those either. Optional stuff just disappeared into the background. I'd think, why bother when I'm already struggling to do the bare minimum? It's not a great mindset, but that's where I was.
Q9: Absolutely. A lot of my studying happened outside the LMS. I'd download lecture audio or videos and listen to them on my phone while lying in bed or taking walks. I'd find YouTube videos on the same topics that explained things differently. Sometimes I'd just think through concepts in my head without any materials at all. The LMS only captured a fraction of what I was actually doing.
Q10: There were many times I'd be processing course material mentally without any digital trace. I'd listen to a downloaded lecture once, then spend hours over the next few days just thinking about it, making connections in my head. Or I'd have conversations with a friend about what I was learning. None of that showed up anywhere. My brain was engaged even when my login activity wasn't.
Q11: Yeah, all the time. I'd access a video or download a PDF once, and then that was it for my LMS activity on that material. But I'd return to those files multiple times offline. It was easier that way, no need to log in, no distractions from the platform. I could just focus on the content itself. The LMS was basically a download portal for me more than a learning environment.
Q12: There were definitely periods where engaging felt almost impossible. I've been dealing with this persistent sadness that makes everything feel muted and pointless. During those times, even logging in felt like climbing a mountain. Nothing specific happened, it was more like an ongoing fog. The course didn't change, but my capacity to care about it would just disappear for stretches of time.
Q13: Fatigue was the big one for me. I'd be tired all the time, not physically exhausted but mentally drained. That made everything harder. I didn't have competing responsibilities exactly, but I also didn't have much driving me forward. The confusion wasn't about the material, it was more about why I should bother. That internal struggle definitely affected how much I used the LMS.
Q14: Oh, plenty of times. I'd open a video, let it play, and realize twenty minutes later that I hadn't absorbed a single thing. My mind would wander to darker places, and the lecture would just be background noise. I was technically present, logged in, watching, but I wasn't really there. It's a strange feeling, going through the motions without any real engagement happening inside.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they mattered. It wasn't procrastination in the fun, distracted way. It was more like paralysis. I'd look at an assignment, know I could probably do it well, and still not start. The gap between knowing and doing felt enormous. By the time I'd gather enough energy, deadlines had often passed. It's frustrating because I knew better.
Q16: Deadlines would create small spikes in my activity, but they weren't reliable motivators for me. Sometimes a deadline would light a fire and I'd actually submit something strong. Other times, I'd watch the deadline pass and feel nothing but a dull resignation. The LMS would show me logging in around due dates, but whether I actually produced anything was hit or miss.
Q17: When I did submit work and got feedback, especially positive feedback, it helped a little. Seeing that I'd done well reminded me that I was capable. But the effect was temporary. The good feeling would fade, and I'd be back to feeling unmotivated. Negative feedback didn't crush me because I have pretty thick skin, but it also didn't push me to do better. I just noted it and moved on.
Q18: The course structure was fairly flexible, which was both good and bad for someone like me. Good because I could work at my own pace when I had energy. Bad because there was nothing forcing me to stay on track. I needed more external structure than the course provided, but I also would have resented it if it had been too rigid. I'm caught in that contradiction.
Q19: The gamified elements were an interesting attempt to make things more engaging, and they did catch my attention briefly. But they didn't address the underlying issue, which was my lack of motivation. Having fourteen courses to juggle also made it hard to focus on any single one. The design wasn't bad, but it couldn't compensate for where I was mentally. Nothing about the platform itself made engagement harder though.
Q20: I think that describes me pretty well, actually. High activity can just mean someone is logging in, browsing, maybe watching videos, but not producing output. For me, the barrier wasn't understanding the material, it was translating that understanding into submitted work. The LMS captured my presence but not my productivity. Someone could be very engaged mentally while still not turning things in.
Q21: That's basically my story. My exam scores and assignment quality were high when I actually submitted, even though my overall activity looked low. I learn efficiently when I focus, and I do a lot of processing outside the system. The LMS data would make me look like a struggling student, but my actual performance tells a different story. Low activity doesn't always mean low learning.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of access, timing, frequency, that kind of thing. They're good at tracking clicks and time spent. But they miss everything that happens in someone's head or outside the platform. They can't measure understanding, they can't see offline study, and they definitely can't capture emotional state. A log might show I watched a video, but not that I was crying while it played.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and wanting to understand it deeply. It's not about how many times you log in or how many posts you make. For me, engagement happened in quiet moments of genuine curiosity, when something clicked and I wanted to know more. Those moments were rare this semester, but they were real. Engagement is internal, not performative.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I was struggling emotionally throughout this course. It wouldn't capture the sadness that made everything feel pointless, or the resilience that kept me coming back despite that. It wouldn't show the hours I spent thinking about course concepts while staring at the ceiling. My engagement was real but invisible. The data tells a story, but it's missing the most important chapters.
